novel_features.name: Reverse ability search
novel_features.description: Find every Pokémon with a given ability. Live API has no reverse index — this is a single SQL query against the local store.
novel_features.rationale: Needs a pokemon ↔ abilities join across the full Pokédex; the live API only exposes ability → named-list, not ability → full pokemon rows.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this when an agent needs to enumerate Pokémon by trait without making thousands of API calls.

novel_features.name: Reverse move-effect search
novel_features.description: Find moves by status effect, damage class, type, or target — e.g. all moves that paralyze a Steel-type.
novel_features.rationale: Joins moves.meta.ailment + damage_class + type with damage relations to a target type. No live endpoint composes these filters.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use this for battle planning questions framed by effect rather than by move name.

novel_features.name: Team partner suggester
novel_features.description: Given an in-progress team, score every remaining Pokémon by how well it covers the team's typing gaps. Returns top candidates.
novel_features.rationale: Computes coverage gaps locally, then ranks ~1200 candidate Pokémon in a single pass. Impossible to run live without thousands of API requests.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Best for team-building agents that need objective gap-coverage scoring rather than vibes-based recommendations.

novel_features.name: Move-learnset diff between forms
novel_features.description: Compare the move learnsets of two Pokémon (often regional forms or megas) and surface what each can learn that the other cannot.
novel_features.rationale: Two pokemon rows joined to pokemon_moves locally; live API forces a full fetch of each form plus manual diff.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Useful when an agent needs to argue 'why pick form X over form Y' with concrete move evidence.

novel_features.name: Cross-generation Pokémon timeline
novel_features.description: Show how a Pokémon has changed over generations: type changes, stat changes, ability changes, and which generation it was introduced in.
novel_features.rationale: Stitches pokemon-species → generation, past-types, past-stats, past-abilities. The live API ships these as separate fields; the CLI presents a real timeline.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this when answering historical Pokémon questions ('was Clefairy always a Fairy type?').

novel_features.name: Regional-form comparator
novel_features.description: List every form for a species (e.g. Vulpix vs Alolan Vulpix) with type, stat, and ability deltas inline.
novel_features.rationale: Joins pokemon-species ↔ pokemon-form ↔ pokemon and renders side-by-side; live API requires per-form fetches.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when an agent needs a species-wide answer rather than a single-form payload.

novel_features.name: Evolution-requirement reverse lookup
novel_features.description: Given a target Pokémon, surface the species you would need to evolve and the conditions (item, level, friendship, time of day) to get there.
novel_features.rationale: Searches every evolution_chain locally for chains whose target species matches the input — no live endpoint exposes this direction.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Pick this when the user knows the Pokémon they want and needs the path to it, not the inverse.

novel_features.name: Coverage-gap finder
novel_features.description: List which of the 18 types your in-progress team has neither defensive resistance nor offensive super-effectiveness against.
novel_features.rationale: Computes a gap matrix locally from the team's types and damage_relations table; no live endpoint composes this.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when answering 'what would a hostile team most likely exploit on this lineup?'

novel_features.name: Local FTS search
novel_features.description: Full-text search across Pokémon, moves, abilities, items, and locations — names plus flavor text — with relevance ranking.
novel_features.rationale: SQLite FTS5 over the local store. Works offline. Live API offers only exact named-resource lookups, not ranked text search.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Agent fallback when names are partial or fuzzy; replaces multiple list calls + grep.

novel_features.name: SQL passthrough
novel_features.description: Read-only SQL access to the local store. Power users and agents can compose joins the CLI doesn't expose directly.
novel_features.rationale: The whole local store is a SQLite database. A composable SQL surface is strictly more capable than any pre-baked command.
novel_features.example: pokeapi-pp-cli sql "SELECT id FROM resources WHERE resource_type='pokemon' ORDER BY id LIMIT 10" --json
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this when a one-off question doesn't have a dedicated subcommand.

novel_features.name: Region encounter map
novel_features.description: Render a region-level encounter table joining locations, areas, encounters, and species — every Pokémon you can find in (e.g.) Kanto.
novel_features.rationale: Multi-resource join across location ↔ location-area ↔ encounters ↔ pokemon-species. Doing this live requires ~40 calls per region.
novel_features.why_it_matters: When the question is regional ('what catches in Kanto?') rather than per-Pokémon.

novel_features.name: Damage calculator
novel_features.description: Compute expected damage range for a move from one Pokémon to another, factoring in STAB, type effectiveness, level, and base stats.
novel_features.rationale: Smogon-calc-equivalent. The damage calculator is the most-used competitive Pokémon tool — it answers the only battle-decision question that matters: how hard does this hit? Reads moves + types + stats locally.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this on every battle-planning question framed as 'will it KO?' or 'how hard does X hit Y?'

novel_features.name: Stat ranker
novel_features.description: Rank Pokémon by a base stat (attack, special-attack, speed, hp, etc.), optionally filtered by type. Returns the top N.
novel_features.rationale: Real teambuilding meta question (top special attackers among Ghost-types) that the live API cannot answer in fewer than ~1200 calls. One SQL query locally.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when learning the meta or filling a role on a team where the question is 'who's the best X-type at Y?'

narrative.headline: PokéAPI as a fully offline Pokédex with SQL, full-text search, type math, and a damage calculator no other Pokémon tool ships as a CLI.
narrative.value_prop: Most PokéAPI clients cache one request at a time. This CLI syncs the entire dataset to a local SQLite store and turns it into compound commands the live API can't answer: reverse ability search, move-effect filters, team partner suggestions, evolution-requirement reverse lookups, regional-form comparisons, and a Smogon-calc-style damage calculator. Plus all 98 endpoints, full-text search, and a SQL passthrough for whatever the typed commands don't cover.
narrative.auth_narrative: PokéAPI is a public, read-only API. No keys, no tokens, no login. Run `pokeapi-pp-cli sync` once and you have the whole Pokédex on disk.

quickstart.comment: First — fetch every resource into the local SQLite store. Takes a few minutes; you only do this once per release of the API.
Core agent-friendly profile: types, abilities, stats, key moves — one local query.
Build a balanced team starting from a partial roster. Selects only the high-gravity fields.
Reverse search by effect — moves that paralyze a Steel-type. The live API can't do this in any single call.
Damage calculator — expected damage range factoring in STAB, type effectiveness, level, and stats.

troubleshoots.symptom: Commands like `search` or `sql` return empty results.
troubleshoots.fix: Run `pokeapi-pp-cli sync` first — these commands read from the local store.

troubleshoots.symptom: Sync seems stuck on a single resource.
troubleshoots.fix: PokéAPI is static-hosted and rarely fails, but a flaky network can stall. Re-run `pokeapi-pp-cli sync --resume` to pick up where you left off.
Pipe through `--ascii` for terminal-rendered art, or use `--out path.png` to save the binary.

troubleshoots.symptom: A Pokémon name returns 'not found' but you spelled it right.
troubleshoots.fix: PokéAPI uses dashed slugs — try `mr-mime`, `farfetchd`, or run `pokeapi-pp-cli search "<name>"` for a fuzzy match.

troubleshoots.symptom: The damage calculator's numbers feel low compared to a real battle.
troubleshoots.fix: The calc covers STAB + type + level + base stats. It does not model abilities, items, weather, terrain, or status — those need a full simulator like Pokémon Showdown.

narrative.when_to_use: Reach for this CLI when you want a fully-local, fully-queryable Pokédex. It is ideal for agents answering Pokémon questions without spending live API calls, for battle-planning workflows that combine type math and learnset filtering, and for any reverse-search question (find the move, the ability, the form, the requirement) that the live REST surface doesn't natively answer. Skip it for purely image-driven workflows where another tool already handles sprite rendering.

recipes.title: What does my team leave exposed?
recipes.explanation: Returns every type your team has neither resistance nor super-effective answer for. One local join.

recipes.title: Reverse-search by effect
recipes.command: pokeapi-pp-cli move find --effect paralyze --type-target steel --json --select name,type,power,accuracy
recipes.explanation: Find paralyzing moves that hit Steel — using --select to keep the response narrow for agent context.

recipes.title: Trim a profile to just what an agent needs
recipes.explanation: Use --select with dotted paths to pluck only high-gravity fields out of a deeply nested response — keeps the agent's context light.

recipes.title: Compare two regional forms
recipes.explanation: Side-by-side learnset diff between Vulpix and Alolan Vulpix. One local query instead of two full API fetches.

recipes.title: Custom join via SQL
recipes.command: pokeapi-pp-cli sql "SELECT id FROM resources WHERE resource_type='pokemon-species' ORDER BY id LIMIT 10" --json
recipes.explanation: Drop down to raw SQL when there's no first-class command — read-only, direct local-store access. The local store schema is (id, resource_type, data) with `data` holding the raw JSON payload.
